subject: ap-chemistry
title: Electrochemical Cells & Cell Potential
stimulus:
A student sets up a galvanic cell at 298 K that has an electrode of Ag(s) immersed in a 1.0 M solution of
Ag+(aq) and an electrode of Cr(s) immersed in a 1.0 M solution of Cr3+(aq), as shown in the diagram above.

parts.prompt:
The student measures the voltage of the cell shown above and discovers that it is zero. Identify the missing
component of the cell, and explain its importance for obtaining a nonzero voltage.
Half-Reaction EÂ° (V)
Ag+(aq) + e âˆ’ â†’ Ag(s) +0.80
Cr3+(aq) + 3 e âˆ’ â†’ Cr(s) ?
parts.scoring_notes: The missing component is a salt bridge (or porous disk/membrane). The salt bridge allows ions to migrate between the two half-cells to maintain electrical neutrality. Without a salt bridge, positive charge builds up in one half-cell (where reduction occurs, consuming cations) and negative charge builds up in the other (where oxidation produces ions). This charge buildup prevents further electron flow and results in zero voltage.

scoring_points.description: For identifying the salt bridge and explaining that it maintains charge balance/electrical neutrality: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The salt bridge is missing. The salt bridge allows for the migration of ions to maintain charge balance in each half-cell. 1 point is earned for the correct answer and a valid explanation.

sample_responses.response: The missing component is a salt bridge. Without it, as electrons flow from the Cr half-cell to the Ag half-cell, one solution develops a net positive charge and the other a net negative charge. This charge imbalance stops electron flow. The salt bridge allows ions to migrate between the two solutions to maintain electrical neutrality and sustain a nonzero voltage.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correctly identifies the salt bridge and explains its role in maintaining charge balance to enable continuous electron flow.

sample_responses.response: The missing component is the wire connecting the two electrodes.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point: the external wire is already present (the voltmeter is measuring through it); the salt bridge is the missing internal circuit component.

required_elements: The salt bridge allows ions to migrate between half-cells to maintain electrical neutrality, enabling continued electron flow and a nonzero voltage.
alternatives.correct_example: The missing component is a salt bridge. Without the salt bridge, as electrons flow through the external circuit, one solution becomes positively charged (Ag$^+$ is consumed but no anions enter) and the other becomes negatively charged (Cr$^{3+}$ is produced). This charge imbalance stops electron flow. The salt bridge allows ions to migrate between the half-cells, maintaining electrical neutrality and enabling a continuous current.

wrong_examples: Saying the wire/external circuit is missing â€” the external circuit is already present (otherwise no measurement could be made at all).
Saying the electrodes are missing â€” both Ag and Cr electrodes are described as present.
common_traps: A galvanic cell requires: (1) two electrodes, (2) two solutions, (3) an external circuit for electrons, and (4) a salt bridge for ion migration. The salt bridge completes the internal circuit.

parts.prompt:
The student adds the missing component to the cell and measures
E
to be +1.54 V. As the cell operates,
cell
Ag+ ions are reduced. Use this information and the information in the table above to do the following.
(i) Calculate the value of EÂ° for the half-reaction Cr3+(aq) + 3 e âˆ’ â†’ Cr(s).
(ii) Write the balanced net-ionic equation for the overall reaction that occurs as the cell operates.
(iii) Calculate the value of Î”GÂ° for the overall cell reaction in J/mol
rxn
.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (b)(i): Ag$^+$ is reduced â†’ Ag is the cathode (EÂ° = +0.80 V). Cr is oxidized â†’ Cr is the anode. $EÂ°_{\text{cell}} = EÂ°_{\text{cathode}} - EÂ°_{\text{anode}} = 0.80 - EÂ°(\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr}) = 1.54\text{ V}$. $EÂ°(\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr}) = 0.80 - 1.54 = -0.74\text{ V}$. Part (b)(ii): Cr is oxidized (+3 per Cr, 3e$^-$ per Cr). Ag$^+$ gains 1e$^-$ per Ag. To balance electrons: multiply Ag/Ag$^+$ by 3. Overall: Cr(s) + 3 Ag$^+$(aq) â†’ Cr$^{3+}$(aq) + 3 Ag(s). Part (b)(iii): n = 3 electrons. $\Delta GÂ° = -nFEÂ° = -(3)(96,485\text{ C/mol})(1.54\text{ V}) = -446,000\text{ J/mol} \approx -4.46 \times 10^5\text{ J/mol}$.

scoring_points.description: For the correct EÂ° for Cr$^{3+}$/Cr = âˆ’0.74 V: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $EÂ°_{cell} = EÂ°_{red}(\text{cathode}) - EÂ°_{red}(\text{anode})$; $+1.54\text{ V} = +0.80\text{ V} - x$; $x = +0.80\text{ V} - (+1.54\text{ V}) = -0.74\text{ V}$. 1 point is earned for a correct calculation of $EÂ°_{red}$.

sample_responses.response: $EÂ°_{cell} = EÂ°_{cathode} - EÂ°_{anode}$. Since Ag$^+$ is reduced, Ag is the cathode ($EÂ° = +0.80$ V). $1.54 = 0.80 - EÂ°(\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr})$. Solving: $EÂ°(\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr}) = 0.80 - 1.54 = -0.74$ V.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correctly applies the cell potential formula and rearranges to find the Cr half-reaction reduction potential.

Does not earn the point: the formula is $EÂ°_{cell} = EÂ°_{cathode} - EÂ°_{anode}$, not a simple subtraction of cell potential from cathode; the Cr potential must be negative for Cr to be spontaneously oxidized.

alternatives.correct_example:
$EÂ°_{\text{cell}} = EÂ°_{\text{cathode}} - EÂ°_{\text{anode}}$
$1.54\text{ V} = 0.80\text{ V} - EÂ°(\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr})$
$EÂ°(\text{Cr}^{3+}/\text{Cr}) = 0.80 - 1.54 = -0.74\text{ V}$

wrong_examples: Adding 0.80 + 1.54 to get EÂ° for Cr â€” the formula is EÂ°$_{cell}$ = EÂ°$_{cathode}$ âˆ’ EÂ°$_{anode}$, not the sum.
Getting a positive value for EÂ°(Cr$^{3+}$/Cr) â€” since Cr must be oxidized (anode) for Ag$^+$ to be reduced, Cr's reduction potential must be more negative.
common_traps: $EÂ°_{\text{cell}} = EÂ°_{\text{cathode}} - EÂ°_{\text{anode}}$. Rearrange to find EÂ°$_{\text{anode}}$ = EÂ°$_{\text{cathode}}$ âˆ’ EÂ°$_{\text{cell}}$ = 0.80 âˆ’ 1.54 = âˆ’0.74 V.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced overall net ionic equation: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $3\text{ Ag}^+(aq) + \text{Cr}(s) \rightarrow 3\text{ Ag}(s) + \text{Cr}^{3+}(aq)$. 1 point is earned for the correctly balanced equation.

sample_responses.response: Oxidation: Cr(s) â†’ Cr$^{3+}$(aq) + 3e$^-$ (Ã—1). Reduction: Ag$^+$(aq) + e$^-$ â†’ Ag(s) (Ã—3). Overall: 3 Ag$^+$(aq) + Cr(s) â†’ 3 Ag(s) + Cr$^{3+}$(aq).
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: electrons balanced (3 each side), charges balanced, correct products.
Does not earn the point: equation is not balanced â€” 3 Ag$^+$ are needed to accept the 3 electrons released by one Cr atom.

alternatives.correct_example:
Cr â†’ Cr$^{3+}$ + 3e$^-$ (oxidation, Ã— 1)
Ag$^+$ + e$^-$ â†’ Ag (reduction, Ã— 3)
Overall: Cr(s) + 3 Ag$^+$(aq) â†’ Cr$^{3+}$(aq) + 3 Ag(s)

wrong_examples: Writing Cr + Ag$^+$ â†’ Cr$^{3+}$ + Ag without balancing electrons (need Ã— 3 for Ag).
Reversing the reaction direction â€” Ag$^+$ is reduced (stated in the problem), Cr is oxidized.
common_traps: Balance electrons: Cr loses 3e$^-$; each Ag$^+$ gains 1e$^-$. Multiply the Ag half-reaction by 3 to balance the 3 electrons.

scoring_points.description: For the correct Î”GÂ° using n = 3 and EÂ° = +1.54 V: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $\Delta GÂ° = -nFEÂ° = -\left(\frac{3\text{ mol }e^-}{1\text{ mol}_{rxn}}\right)\left(96{,}485\frac{\text{C}}{\text{mol }e^-}\right)\left(1.54\frac{\text{J}}{\text{C}}\right) = -4.46 \times 10^5\text{ J/mol}_{rxn}$. 1 point is earned for the correct calculation of the value of $\Delta GÂ°$.

sample_responses.response: $\Delta GÂ° = -nFEÂ° = -(3)(96{,}485\text{ C/mol})(1.54\text{ V}) = -4.46 \times 10^5\text{ J/mol}_{rxn}$. The negative sign confirms the reaction is thermodynamically favorable.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correct n = 3, correct formula, correct sign and magnitude.
Does not earn the point: uses n = 1 instead of n = 3; the balanced equation transfers 3 moles of electrons per mole of reaction.

alternatives.correct_example:
$\Delta GÂ° = -nFEÂ° = -(3)(96,485)(1.54) = -446,000\text{ J/mol} = -4.46 \times 10^5\text{ J/mol}_{\text{rxn}}$
(Negative: confirms thermodynamically favorable reaction)

wrong_examples: Using n = 1 â€” 3 electrons are transferred in the balanced equation.
Using n = 6 â€” the total electrons transferred is 3 (not doubled since we used Ã— 1 for Cr and Ã— 3 for Ag, and 3 Ã— 1 = 3).
common_traps: n = total electrons transferred in the balanced equation. Cr loses 3e$^-$ and 3 Ag$^+$ each gain 1e$^-$ = 3e$^-$ total. n = 3.
